errors.humanNameRequired: All human players must have a name
errors.createGame: Error creating the game. Check that the server is running.

subtitles: What you need to achieve and the basic rules.
How to move and what usually gives you an advantage.
What each control means during a game.
Choose your next step or ask what you need.

objective.title: Objective
objective.p1: The main goal is to be the first player to move all ten of your pieces from your starting point (one tip of the star) to the tip directly opposite yours.
objective.p2: The first player to move all ten pieces into the ten spaces of their destination area wins. If two players complete their bases in the same turn, you can consider it a draw or apply a tie-break rule (for example, the player whose turn came first).

norms.title: Rules
norms.item1: The board is a six-point star. At the start, players are created, each assigned a color on the board with ten pieces placed on one tip of the star.
norms.item2: These starting positions are predefined in the system based on how many players participate.
norms.item3: Only one piece moves per turn. You can change the move or even change which piece you move before confirming.
norms.item4: Unlike traditional checkers, in Chinese Checkers you do not capture or remove pieces. All pieces remain in play until the end.
norms.item5: Any player can occupy any cell during the game, except when a piece is already on that position.
norms.item6: Players can block opponents by occupying key spaces. However, you cannot permanently occupy another player’s start or destination base if that prevents them from completing their objective. You may move through them, but your goal is to fill your own destination base.
norms.item7: Players take turns moving one of their pieces. Turn order is predefined by the system and follows the order established when creating the participants. When you create players, the order they appear is the same order used during the game.

moves.title: Types of moves
moves.simpleDesc: A piece may move to an adjacent empty space in any of the six possible directions (forward, backward, sideways, or diagonally).
moves.jumpDesc: A piece may jump over another piece (yours or an opponent’s) if there is an empty space immediately on the other side. The jump must be in a straight line.
moves.chainDesc: After a jump, if the piece can make another jump, it may do so in the same turn. Multiple jumps are not mandatory but can be very advantageous. Jumps may change direction each time.
moves.simpleAlt: Example of a single move to an adjacent cell
moves.jumpAlt: Example of a straight-line jump over a piece
moves.chainAlt: Example of a jump chain with several jumps in one turn

strategy.title: Strategy tips
strategy.item1: Chaining jumps lets you advance much faster than single moves.
strategy.item2: Place pieces so they can support jumps in future turns.
strategy.item3: Lone pieces advance slower because they cannot take advantage of jumps.
strategy.item4: Keeping your pieces relatively close makes moves more efficient.
strategy.item5: You can also jump over their pieces, so keep an eye on the whole board.
strategy.item6: Don’t focus only on the immediate move; try to prepare jump chains for upcoming turns.

ui.title: What does each screen element mean?
ui.intro: A game is made up of several elements. Let’s explain them below.

board.title: Board
board.p1: As explained before, the board is a six-point star: each tip has an assigned color and a player. It is in the center of the screen and is the main element of the game.
board.p2: To move a piece, first select the piece you want to move and then the destination. The system will show available cells based on your chosen move so you can confirm clearly before continuing.

rounds.title: Round and players
rounds.p1: This panel appears on the left side and shows whose turn it is, each participant’s color, and the current round number. It also indicates whether each player is human or AI and, for AI, its difficulty (Easy or Hard). Use it to plan your moves and clearly see who plays next.
rounds.alt: Rounds panel with current turn and players

timer.title: Timer
timer.p1: It is at the top of the screen. It shows the elapsed game time. When the game is paused the time stops counting, so it reflects active play time. There is no time limit per move or for finishing the game.

ui.topControlsAria: Top controls for music and pause

music.title: Music
music.p1: It is located to the left of the timer. Clicking it enables or disables the game music.

pause.title: Pause
pause.p1: To the right of the timer you have the pause access. From there you can open the pause menu and also return home using the Home button when needed.
pause.homeAlt: Home button in the pause panel

pausePanel.title: Resume / Cancel
pausePanel.p1: Pressing Pause opens this panel. You can choose Resume to continue exactly where you left off, or Cancel to end the game permanently. If you cancel, the game is deleted and cannot be resumed.
pausePanel.alt: Paused game screen with Resume and Finish buttons

undo.title: Undo
undo.p1: After moving a piece, you can press “Undo” to revert the move and choose another piece or route before confirming.

passRound.title: Pass round
passRound.p1: If you don’t want to move any piece this turn, press “Pass Round” and the turn moves to the next player automatically.

continue.title: Continue
continue.p1: After selecting and moving your piece, press “Continue” to confirm the move and hand the turn to the next player.

help.title: Help
help.p1: This section on the right opens an assistant to answer questions during the game. You can ask, for example: how to move a piece in a concrete situation, when it’s convenient to chain jumps, what each UI button does, what Pause/Resume/Cancel means, how the current round is calculated, or what basic strategy fits your current position.
help.p2: Pressing “Open Help” shows the assistant panel, an info button with question examples, and a text area to write and send your query. Each game keeps its own help context so answers are useful for the current moment.
help.alt: Help panel with assistant and Send button

finish.title: End of tutorial
finish.intro: You’ve reached the end of the tutorial. What would you like to do now?

quick.label: Quick game:
quick.desc: A match where you’ll play against an Easy opponent.

demo.label: Demo:
demo.desc: A match with two players facing each other on Hard difficulty.

chatbot.label: Chatbot:
chatbot.desc: If you still have questions before playing, you can ask our conversational agent at the end of this screen.

chat.title: CheckerIT chatbot
chat.hint: Ask concrete and short questions, such as game rules, moves, or the interface. If your query is too long, shorten it so the chatbot can respond better.
suggestedQuestions: How do you move a piece?
How do you win a game?
What does the Pause button do?

errors.tooLong: Your question is too long. Please shorten it, for example: "game rules", "how to move a piece" or "what pause does".
errors.tooLongShort: Your question is too long. Try shortening it, for example: "game rules" or "how to move a piece".
errors.timeout: The response is taking too long. Try a shorter question, like "game rules" or "how to move a piece".
errors.send: Error sending message to the chatbot.

help.title: Assistant
help.placeholder: Type a question and press Send.

examples.title: Example questions:
questions: "What are the rules of the game?"
"How can I cancel or finish a game?"
"What does the Pause button do and how do I resume?"

errors.send: Error sending message to the chatbot

confirm.title: Finish Game
confirm.question: Are you sure you want to end it early?
confirm.warning: This action cannot be undone. You will lose the progress you have made in this game

warnings.invalidMove: Invalid move: you must move to an empty adjacent cell or jump over a piece to an empty cell
